context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, i’m working on a 700–800 word reflection on development as freedom, and i’m kinda stuck on what the “added value” part really means. can u help me figure out how to answer that without making it too vague?
exchanges.tutor: Good question to start with. Before thinking about what 'freedom' adds, what do you think development was primarily measured by *before* thinkers like Amartya Sen reframed it? What was the dominant definition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help understanding what 'added value' means in the context of the prompt. Rather than explaining it directly, I should use a Socratic question to get them thinking about what development looked like *before* the 'freedom' framing, so they can identify the contrast and derive the added value themselves. This is an early exchange, so I want to keep scaffolding minimal and let them do the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: i think mostly economic growth, like GDP/income and maybe industrialization? idk if i’m missing another big one tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's right, economic growth and income were the dominant measures. So now think about this: what can a person have in terms of income or economic output, but still *lack* in terms of freedom? Try to think of a concrete example.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the main prior framework (GDP/income/economic growth). I should affirm this briefly and then push them to make the contrast themselves - what does freedom as development capture that GDP doesn't? This is the core of the 'added value' question and they should arrive at it through their own reasoning.

exchanges.student: maybe someone can have a job/income but still not have education, healthcare, or political voice? like GDP can rise while people still can’t really choose how to live.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly - that tension is key. So given that gap you just identified, what would it mean in practice for a development *planner* to use freedom as their goal rather than GDP growth? How might their priorities or decisions look different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a strong intuitive leap connecting material wealth to the absence of substantive freedoms. This is essentially the core of Sen's 'capabilities' argument. I should affirm this and now push them to connect it directly to the 'added value' question - what does this mean for how we *plan* development? This moves the conversation toward the second part of the prompt about planning implications.

exchanges.student: i think they’d focus more on what people are actually able to do, not just output numbers. so maybe priorities shift to health, education, participation, and reducing barriers, not just building stuff for growth.
exchanges.tutor: Good, you're building a strong case for how it *helps*. But the prompt also asks whether it might *hinder* planning. Can you think of any reasons why using freedom as the goal could make a planner's job harder or more complicated?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is building a solid argument. They've now identified both what's added (capabilities, participation, health, education) and how it changes planning priorities. Rather than just affirming and moving on, I should push them to also consider the tension in the prompt - 'will it help OR hinder development planning?' They've only addressed the 'help' side so far. This is an important critical dimension of the reflection they'll need to address.

exchanges.student: yeah, kinda—“freedom” feels broad and hard to measure, so planners might not know what to prioritize. also different groups may want diff freedoms, which could create conflict tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Those are real tensions worth keeping in your reflection. Now, the prompt also asks about the relationship between 'development as freedom' and *rights-based* development planning. How do you think those two ideas connect - are they basically the same thing, or is there a meaningful difference?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two genuine tensions: measurability and conflicting priorities among groups. These are solid critical points. Now I want to push them toward the third and fourth parts of the prompt - the rights-based development connection and the 'who gets to exercise the right to development' question. We're about halfway through the planned conversation length, so I should start steering toward those remaining parts of the prompt.

exchanges.student: i don’t think they’re exactly the same. they overlap bc both care about dignity and what people can actually access, but rights-based planning sounds more legal/political, like claims and duties, while freedom is a broader idea about capabilities.
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp distinction. So with that in mind, let's get to the last part of the prompt - who actually gets to *exercise* the right to development? Think about the actors involved: individuals, states, international organizations. Who holds the power to claim or grant that right, and does everyone have equal access to it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely sharp distinction between the two frameworks - capabilities vs. rights as legal claims. This is a sophisticated observation. I should affirm it briefly and push them toward the final and arguably most politically charged part of the prompt: 'who gets to exercise the right to development?' This question is about power and legitimacy, which is a core theme of the course.

exchanges.student: i’m thinking individuals should hold it, but states kinda control whether it’s real in practice, and intl orgs/donors shape it too. so no, access def isn’t equal, esp for marginalized groups or weaker states.
exchanges.tutor: You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. You have real substance to write with. Before we go further, I'd encourage you to try drafting a few sentences - even just a rough paragraph - that pulls these ideas together into an argument. What would your central claim be for this reflection?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has touched on a critical power dynamic that connects well to the course's themes of power, legitimacy, and external actors in development planning. They've identified the gap between who *should* hold rights and who actually controls them in practice. This is good material. We've now covered all four parts of the prompt across our exchanges. Given we're around exchange 7-8, I should now push the student to start integrating all of this into a written attempt, as per the redundancy/integration rule and the conversation length guideline.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe my claim is that thinking of development as freedom adds value bc it shifts attention from growth alone to people’s real capabilities and choices, but it also makes planning messier bc freedom is harder to define and measure. and rights-based planning is related but more about enforceable claims and state duties, while the right to development is unequally exercised bc power is uneven across people and countries.
exchanges.tutor: That covers the ground well. One thing to consider: right now it reads as a list of points. For a reflection, ask yourself - is there a through-line that connects all of these ideas into one coherent argument? What is the *so what* that ties the capability gap, the planning challenges, and the power question together?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear, multi-part central claim that addresses all four parts of the prompt. This is good progress. Rather than just affirming it, I should push them to think about whether this claim has a clear enough argumentative thread - does it just list points or does it build toward something? I should also remind them of the word count and that a reflection needs more than a list of observations. I want to push them toward thinking about structure and coherence without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: yeah i see that, mine sounds list-y rn. maybe the through-line is that “development as freedom” is valuable bc it exposes how development is really about power over life chances, not just growth?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong through-line - it gives your whole reflection a spine. Now go write the draft. Keep that central idea visible to yourself as you write each paragraph. Come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely strong through-line that connects capabilities, planning challenges, rights, and power into one coherent argument. This is a meaningful intellectual leap and worth affirming. Since we're near the end of the planned conversation length, I should encourage them to now go write their draft with this through-line in mind, rather than continuing to refine the argument in chat.

exchanges.student: thanks, that actually helps a lot. if i draft an intro later, can i bring it back for a quick check?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely, bring it back. Just keep in mind I won't edit or rewrite anything for you - but I can ask questions that help you see where your argument is strong or where it needs more work. Good luck with the draft!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a reasonable and appropriate follow-up question about continuing the tutoring process. I should welcome this while reminding them that I won't be editing or polishing their work - I'll engage with it through questions and feedback, not corrections.
